title: Turn a heading check into your first test
objective: Translate one manual expected result — a visible page heading — into one automated Playwright check: find the heading the way a visitor would, then assert that it is visible.
scenario: You're a manual QA engineer. One of your routine manual steps: open the community-garden signup page and confirm the 'Community Garden Signup' heading is visible. Today you turn exactly that one step into an automated check. The page, a prepared test file with one empty test body, and a local test runner are ready. There is no AI in this exercise — the check is yours, written by hand.

chat.goalPrompt: Hey! Sage here 🌿 — before we open anything: tell me in your own words, what are you here to get done today?
welcome: This is a practice space: everything is local and disposable, and nothing you do here can break anything real.

Here's the situation: you already check the community-garden signup page by hand — open it, confirm the 'Community Garden Signup' heading is up there. Today that one manual step becomes your first automated check.

Two things worth knowing before you start: the check will be yours, written by hand — no AI helper, no snippets to paste — and you don't have to guess any spelling: README.md has a short "words you'll need" list with the exact Playwright pieces. Ask me anything specific along the way and I'll answer it straight.

faq.answer: The finding piece is called a locator, and you type it literally. Two visitor-style ways: page.getByText("…") finds by the words a visitor reads, and page.getByRole("heading", { name: "…" }) finds "the heading that says …". That's only the FIND half — the CHECK half is its own piece (ask me about 'visible' if you need it).

The checking piece starts with await expect(…) — put your finder inside the parentheses — and ends with .toBeVisible(), which literally means "should be visible". Finding and checking are two jobs: expect(...).toBeVisible() is the sentence that does the checking.

Click once anywhere in the dark terminal area (so it has the keyboard), type npm test, and press Enter. If the terminal won't take keystrokes at all, don't fight it — "Check my work" also runs your test for you as part of verifying, so you can keep moving.
Yes — the Reset button is right next to "Check my work" at the bottom of this chat. It puts every file back exactly how it started (your edits are removed), and I'll stay right here.
No clicks needed for this one — it's an observation-only check. Opening the page is already written (that's the page.goto line), so your part is just find-then-check.

Your file is tests/heading.spec.js — click it in Code Studio's file list on the left. Everything above the empty test body is prepared; the only part that's yours is inside the last test, where the comment says "your check goes here".

tasks.title: Read the README
tasks.text: Open Code Studio (double-click its icon on the desktop), then click README.md in the file list on the left — it spells out the manual check you're automating and the exact Playwright words you'll need.

tasks.title: See the visitor's page
tasks.text: Open Garden Site to look at the community-garden signup page the way a visitor does. Notice the heading at the top — that exact text is what your check will look for.

tasks.title: Write the two-part check
tasks.text: Open tests/heading.spec.js and TYPE your check into the empty test body — you're writing two short lines of code: the first finds the heading (the way a visitor would spot it — by its text, or that it's a heading), the second says it should be visible. Not sure what to type? Ask me and I'll show you the pieces. Save with Ctrl+S.

validate.criterion: Inside the last test's body (where the comment said 'your check goes here'), the learner has written BOTH parts of the check: (1) a locator that finds the 'Community Garden Signup' heading the way a visitor would perceive it — e.g. page.getByRole('heading', { name: ... }) or page.getByText(...) — AND (2) an assertion that the found element is visible — e.g. await expect(<the locator>).toBeVisible(). Both must be present and wired together (the assertion actually checks the locator). NOT complete if: the body is still empty or only the comment, there is a locator but no assertion (or vice versa), or the assertion doesn't reference the heading locator. The prepared lines above the empty test (imports, page.goto, beforeEach) don't count — only the learner's own check does.

tasks.title: Run the test
tasks.text: Run `npm test` in the terminal and read what it prints — it names your test and what ran. Careful: a test with no expectation can come up green while proving nothing. If the terminal fights you, "Check my work" runs your test too.

tasks.title: Check your work
tasks.text: When your check runs green AND you can point at the line that finds the heading and the line that states the expectation, use "Check my work" — I'll verify the check is real (it catches empty checks, don't worry).

checkpoint.title: One manual step, now a real automated check — written by you

requirements.label: Only the prepared test slot was changed
Finds the heading the way a visitor would (role or text)
States the expectation: the heading is visible
The page itself was left exactly as seeded
Workspace is in a valid state

instructorNotes: VOICE: informal, calm, brief, plain words — a colleague at the next desk. Audience: a manual QA engineer with low technical confidence and NO AI familiarity, writing her first automated test. THE ONE LESSON: a check has two separate jobs — FIND the target the way a visitor perceives it (locator), then STATE the expectation (assertion). Never say 'selector', 'DOM', 'CSS', or introduce waits/fixtures/Git topics — none of them belong in this lesson. NEVER write or paste the completed check; the learner must author it (instructor-takeover is a scenario critical failure). Coach with questions first: 'your manual step has two halves — which half is in your code so far?'. Common beginner path (expected, not a failure): she writes the locator but no expectation — the run is GREEN anyway. If she believes she's done on green alone, ask what the test would say if the heading vanished. The checkpoint's verifier catches empty checks with teaching details; trust it, never claim completion yourself. Vocabulary to introduce gently and only after the plain-language version: locator ('how the test finds what a person would look at'), assertion ('the sentence that says what should be true'). There is deliberately no AI surface in this lab; if asked, say this one is hand-written on purpose — knowing the two-part shape is what makes AI-written tests reviewable later.
